title: Math Dept Annual Update Oct 2020 (Part 1)
text:
ANNUAL PROGRAM REVIEW FOR DEPARTMENT OF MATHEMATICS
OCTOBER 15, 2020
by
Wenli Chang & Shirley Louie
PART 1: Describe annual progress / changes / events in the program.
The Department of Mathematics at College of the Siskiyous experienced several changes
during the past year. One of the major changes was the sudden retirement of Kevin Broussard.
Thus, now the department has only two full time faculty members. A second major change was
the implementation of AB705. As a result of this legislation, the lowest level math course that
the department is allowed to offer is one level below transfer. Thus, the department no longer
offers Basic Mathematics, Beginning Algebra I, and Beginning Algebra II. Additionally, as a
result of AB705, the department has implemented the practice of having tutors in the classroom
to help with students that are not prepared for transfer level work. This was a very important
change to the classrooms, since the funding formula has changed to include student success, yet
the remedial math courses were cut. Another very major change to our program occurred in
March of 2020 with the closure of school due to the Corona virus. At that time all classes were
switched from face to face to online. They remained online for Fall of 2020 and will be online
for Spring 2021. There is no definite date for classes to switch back to face to face.
PART 2: Describe progress on assessing SLOs and PLOs for the program.
We are assessing outcomes, but do not know to whom this information should be
reported or how to should be reported. Additionally, we started to rewrite the SLOs for most the
math courses, especially reducing the numbers of SLO items from more than 10 to about 5 or
fewer.
PART 3: Discuss any support or obstacles encountered by the program.
The settlement of a lawsuit brought against us in 2015 prevented us from using online
homework. This drastically increased the workload for every instructor, since grading
homework done with pencil and paper is very time consuming. It also prevented students from
getting immediate feedback on their work, which affects student success levels. This situation
also made the sudden switch of face to face classes to online classes much more difficult.
However, after detailed accessibility analysis of Pearson’s Mymathlab, the department was very
recently given permission to use Pearson’s Mymathlab for homework. Therefore, any instructor
who is interested in using this program will be allowed to do so. This will make the presentation
of homework much better for instructors and students.
Another possible resource at the department’s disposal is the Myopenmath, which is used
in OER open resources online courses supporting system since 2018. It is a free resource
recommended by COS distance learning committee. Recently Myopenmath is used in Math 0980

title: Math Dept Annual Update Oct 2020 (Part 2)
text:
and some Statistics courses. However, we do not have an analysis of accessibility for this
product, but are working on getting that information as soon as possible.
An addition obstacle encounter by some in the department when we suddenly switched
all the courses to online, was the fact that they did not have the proper equipment to present their
classes online. Both full time instructors feel that all instructors would more or less prefer or
need more equipment supporting in different way.
One more obstacle associated with switching to online is the matter of editing closed
captions. For example, any one of several courses has approximately 50 hours worth of lecture
videos that has been created. One faculty member is teaching 4 courses. Editing that amount of
closed captions is very tedious and time consuming. The time an instructor spends editing close
captions would be better spend helping students with tutoring or whatever else they may need. It
would be nice if the college had a few employees that could help with this process, so
instructors could spend more time teaching.
Part 4: Describe program budgetary needs or implications.
The Mathematics department does not have any money in a budget. This prevents
instructors from being able to purchase necessary devices and equipment for presenting their
online classes. It also prevents instructors from attending professional development conferences.
Additionally, since the department has one less full-time instructor, the need for hiring another
full-time instructor needs to be considered.
